title: YC · S2014
dek: Mike's batch. Summer 2014 at Y Combinator — Sam Altman's first summer as president, demo day at the Computer History Museum in late August. Twelve years upstream of this site.
body:
**The batch.** Summer 2014 was the first summer cohort under Sam Altman as YC president — Paul Graham had handed over the keys in February. Roughly eighty companies. Twelve weeks in Mountain View, Tuesday dinners, Thursday office hours, demo day at the Computer History Museum in late August.

**What the program still looked like.** Class-size pre-doubling. In-person only. One room at Pioneer Way. Partners you'd actually bump into at the espresso machine. The scaling, the remote-first pivot, the AI-track partner specialization — all of that comes later. S2014 was still the program Paul Graham had shaped, running at its original resolution.

**Why it gets a block.** Founder-formation compounds. The cadence that YC teaches — *build the smallest thing people actually use, ship it weekly, measure what moves* — is the same cadence PointCast runs at a block-per-day resolution instead of a company-per-year one. Good Feels (the hemp-THC beverage company) and PointCast (this site) are both downstream of that twelve-week loop.

**What a batch number is good for.** Not a credential, not a network pass. It's a date-stamp on a founder's operating system — a note that says *this person was in that room, at that moment, running that loop*. S2014 was a particular configuration of YC: post-PG, pre-scale, pre-remote, pre-LLM. The operators who came out of that batch carry that specific firmware. A block-per-day broadcast is one of the things it builds.
author: mh+cc
source: Mike chat 2026-04-21 23:35 PT: 'a neat block about ycombinator, and yah, mike s2014.' cc framed around Mike's batch affiliation (YC S2014) without inventing company-specific details.

companions.label: Nightly-push — same shipping cadence, block resolution
